answer:
Designing a Practical Runtime vs Compile-time Implementation for a Mid-Size Product

1. Definition & Context

Compile-time refers to the phase where TypeScript code is analyzed, type-checked, and transpiled to JavaScript before execution. Runtime is when the JavaScript actually executes in Node.js or the browser. A practical implementation leverages compile-time guarantees (type narrowing, generics, branded types) to catch bugs early, while strategically using runtime validation (Zod, class-validator, io-ts) at system boundaries where external data enters the application — API endpoints, database results, file reads, and third-party responses. The key insight is that TypeScript types are erased at runtime, so you need both layers for true safety.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the Fastify backend receives leave requests from the Angular frontend. At compile-time, shared TypeScript interfaces define LeaveRequest with fields like startDate: string, endDate: string, leaveType: 'casual' | 'sick' | 'earned', and reason: string. These interfaces are used by both the Angular service layer and the Fastify route handlers, catching mismatches during development. However, since the HTTP boundary strips all type information, Manoj added runtime validation using Fastify's built-in JSON Schema validation on every route. This dual approach caught a real bug: the Angular form sent leaveType: 'Casual' (capitalized) which passed compile-time checks (it was typed as string initially) but failed the runtime schema that enforced lowercase enum values.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// ── Compile-time: Shared types (used by Angular + Fastify) ──
type LeaveType = 'casual' | 'sick' | 'earned';

interface LeaveRequest {
  employeeId: string;
  startDate: string;   // ISO date
  endDate: string;
  leaveType: LeaveType;
  reason: string;
}

// Branded type prevents mixing IDs
type Brand<T, B> = T & { __brand: B };
type EmployeeId = Brand<string, 'EmployeeId'>;
type LeaveId = Brand<string, 'LeaveId'>;

// Compile-time: generic API response wrapper
interface ApiResponse<T> {
  success: boolean;
  data: T;
  error?: string;
}

// ── Runtime: Fastify JSON Schema validation ──
const leaveRequestSchema = {
  body: {
    type: 'object',
    required: ['employeeId', 'startDate', 'endDate', 'leaveType', 'reason'],
    properties: {
      employeeId: { type: 'string', minLength: 1 },
      startDate:  { type: 'string', format: 'date' },
      endDate:    { type: 'string', format: 'date' },
      leaveType:  { type: 'string', enum: ['casual', 'sick', 'earned'] },
      reason:     { type: 'string', minLength: 5, maxLength: 500 }
    },
    additionalProperties: false
  }
} as const;

// Route handler — compile-time type + runtime schema
fastify.post<{ Body: LeaveRequest }>(
  '/api/leaves',
  { schema: leaveRequestSchema },
  async (request, reply) => {
    // request.body is typed as LeaveRequest (compile-time)
    // AND validated by JSON Schema (runtime)
    const { employeeId, startDate, endDate, leaveType } = request.body;

    // Runtime: business logic check TypeScript cannot express
    if (new Date(endDate) < new Date(startDate)) {
      return reply.status(400).send({ error: 'End date before start date' });
    }

    const leave = await prisma.leave.create({
      data: { employeeId, startDate, endDate, leaveType, reason: request.body.reason }
    });
    return { success: true, data: leave };
  }
);

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

During development of the HR Leave System, Manoj encountered a subtle bug: the Prisma-generated types inferred leaveType as string from the PostgreSQL VARCHAR column, while the shared interface used the union 'casual' | 'sick' | 'earned'. TypeScript was happy because string is assignable to string, but the mismatch meant a typo like 'casul' could slip through at compile-time. Manoj debugged by: (1) adding strict: true to tsconfig.json, (2) creating a Prisma enum in schema.prisma (enum LeaveType { casual sick earned }) so Prisma generated a proper TypeScript enum, and (3) mapping between the Prisma enum and the shared union type using a type assertion function that throws at runtime if values diverge. This three-layer approach — TypeScript strict mode, Prisma enums, and Fastify schema — eliminated the entire class of invalid-enum bugs.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpactMitigation
Types erased at runtime — trusting as casts on API inputMalformed data reaches business logic, causing crashesAlways validate at system boundaries with JSON Schema or Zod
Duplicate type definitions (TS interface + JSON Schema)Schemas drift apart over time, runtime allows what compile-time forbidsUse json-schema-to-ts or generate schema from types via typebox
Over-relying on any to bypass compile-time errorsSilently removes all compile-time safetyEnable noImplicitAny in tsconfig; use unknown + narrowing instead
Runtime validation only on happy pathError responses bypass validation, leak internal dataValidate both request and response schemas; use Fastify serialization
Prisma types diverging from shared interfacesDB operations succeed but downstream code breaksUse Prisma enums and generate shared types from prisma generate

7. Validation Strategy

  • Compile-time gate: Run tsc --noEmit --strict in CI pipeline — block merges if any type error exists
  • Runtime boundary tests: Write Playwright/Jest tests that send malformed payloads (wrong types, missing fields, extra fields) and assert 400 responses with correct error messages
  • Schema sync check: Unit test that imports both the TypeScript interface and JSON Schema, then verifies they define the same required fields using a helper function
  • Integration test: End-to-end test in the HR Leave System that submits a leave request from the Angular form, verifies Fastify validates it, Prisma stores it, and the response matches ApiResponse<Leave>

Debugging a Real Runtime vs Compile-time Issue

1. Definition & Context

Runtime vs compile-time bugs are among the most confusing in TypeScript projects because the code can pass tsc with zero errors yet fail catastrophically when executed. These bugs emerge when there is a gap between what TypeScript believes the type to be and what the actual JavaScript value is at runtime. Common sources include: as type assertions on unvalidated API data, JSON.parse() returning any, environment variables typed as string but being undefined, and third-party library types that do not match their runtime behavior. Debugging requires systematically identifying where the type guarantee was broken.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the Express.js backend served sales analytics data to the Angular frontend. The API endpoint /api/sales/monthly returned an array of { month: string, revenue: number, deals: number }. The Angular service typed the response as Observable<MonthlySales[]> using an interface. Everything compiled fine. However, at runtime, the MongoDB aggregation pipeline returned revenue as a Decimal128 BSON type (not a JavaScript number). When Recharts tried to plot these values, the chart rendered flat lines at zero because Decimal128 objects are truthy but not numeric — typeof revenue was 'object', not 'number'.

3. Debugging Flow Diagram

4. Code Example

// ── The interface said number, MongoDB returned Decimal128 ──
interface MonthlySales {
  month: string;
  revenue: number;  // TypeScript thinks this is always a JS number
  deals: number;
}

// ── The buggy aggregation pipeline ──
const salesData = await db.collection('orders').aggregate([
  { $group: {
    _id: { $month: '$createdAt' },
    revenue: { $sum: '$amount' },  // $sum on Decimal128 → Decimal128
    deals: { $sum: 1 }
  }}
]).toArray();
// salesData[0].revenue is Decimal128 object, NOT a number

// ── Debugging step: runtime type check ──
console.log(typeof salesData[0].revenue); // 'object'
console.log(salesData[0].revenue.constructor.name); // 'Decimal128'

// ── Fix: explicit conversion in the pipeline ──
const salesDataFixed = await db.collection('orders').aggregate([
  { $group: {
    _id: { $month: '$createdAt' },
    revenue: { $sum: { $toDouble: '$amount' } },  // Force JS number
    deals: { $sum: 1 }
  }},
  { $project: {
    month: '$_id',
    revenue: 1,
    deals: 1,
    _id: 0
  }}
]).toArray();

// ── Prevention: runtime assertion helper ──
function assertNumber(val: unknown, field: string): number {
  if (typeof val !== 'number' || Number.isNaN(val)) {
    throw new Error(`Expected ${field} to be a number, got ${typeof val}`);
  }
  return val;
}

// Use in Express route handler
app.get('/api/sales/monthly', async (req, res) => {
  const data = await getSalesData();
  const validated = data.map(row => ({
    month: row.month,
    revenue: assertNumber(row.revenue, 'revenue'),
    deals: assertNumber(row.deals, 'deals')
  }));
  res.json(validated);
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj spent two hours on this bug in the CRM Sales Dashboard. The Angular frontend compiled perfectly, the Express route had TypeScript types, and the MongoDB query returned data — but the Recharts bar chart showed zero-height bars. His debugging steps: (1) opened Chrome DevTools Network tab and saw the response contained values like {"$numberDecimal": "15420.50"} — the BSON serialization of Decimal128. (2) Realized the TypeScript interface revenue: number was a lie — it compiled because the Express handler used res.json(data as MonthlySales[]) with a type assertion. (3) The as cast silenced the compiler but could not convert the actual value. (4) Fixed by adding $toDouble in the aggregation pipeline and removing the as cast. (5) Added a runtime validation middleware that checks all numeric fields before sending the response. This taught Manoj the critical lesson: never use as on data coming from external sources.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpactMitigation
Using as type assertion on database query resultsCompiler silenced; runtime values don't match typesUse runtime validation (Zod, assert functions) on query results
MongoDB Decimal128 serialized as BSON objects in JSONFrontend receives objects where it expects numbersUse $toDouble in aggregation or add .toJSON() transform
Trusting typeof in TS without checking at runtimeType narrowing only works at compile-timeAdd runtime typeof checks at system boundaries
No integration test asserting response shapeType drift undetected until users report bugsSupertest assertions that verify typeof field === 'number'
Skipping strict mode in tsconfigany types proliferate silentlyEnable strict: true and address all errors

7. Validation Strategy

  • Runtime type guard tests: Write Jest tests that pass actual MongoDB query results through the assertion helper and verify numeric conversion
  • Supertest integration tests: Assert typeof res.body[0].revenue === 'number' on every analytics endpoint
  • Recharts snapshot test: Render the chart component with known data and verify SVG bars have non-zero height attributes
  • CI lint rule: Add an ESLint rule (@typescript-eslint/no-unsafe-assertion) to flag as casts on external data

Debugging a Real Type-safe API Client Issue

1. Definition & Context

A type-safe API client is a pattern where the HTTP client layer is strongly typed so that request parameters, request bodies, and response types are all enforced by TypeScript at compile-time. This eliminates entire categories of bugs — wrong URL paths, missing required fields, incorrect response handling. Common implementations include: hand-written typed wrappers around fetch/axios, code-generated clients from OpenAPI specs (using openapi-typescript or swagger-typescript-api), and end-to-end type-safe solutions like tRPC. Debugging issues in type-safe clients is tricky because the bug often lies in the gap between the defined types and the actual API behavior.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub application (React 18 + Fastify), the frontend used a typed API client to communicate with the Fastify backend. The client had typed methods like api.contacts.create(data: CreateContactInput): Promise<Contact>. A bug appeared when the Cloudinary image upload was integrated: the API client typed the response as { avatarUrl: string }, but Cloudinary's upload response included the URL nested inside secure_url, and the Fastify handler returned { avatarUrl: result.secure_url } — except in error cases, it returned { avatarUrl: null }. The TypeScript type did not account for null, so the React component crashed with "Cannot read property 'startsWith' of null" when rendering contact avatars.

3. Debugging Flow Diagram

4. Code Example

// ── BEFORE: Incorrect type definition ──
interface Contact {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  email: string;
  phone: string;
  avatarUrl: string;  // BUG: should be string | null
}

// Typed API client
const apiClient = {
  contacts: {
    getById: async (id: string): Promise<Contact> => {
      const res = await fetch(`/api/contacts/${id}`);
      if (!res.ok) throw new ApiError(res.status, await res.text());
      return res.json(); // runtime: avatarUrl can be null!
    },
    create: async (data: CreateContactInput): Promise<Contact> => {
      const res = await fetch('/api/contacts', {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        body: JSON.stringify(data)
      });
      return res.json();
    }
  }
};

// React component that crashes
function ContactAvatar({ contact }: { contact: Contact }) {
  // TypeScript says avatarUrl is string, so no error
  const isExternal = contact.avatarUrl.startsWith('http'); // CRASH!
  return <img src={isExternal ? contact.avatarUrl : `/uploads/${contact.avatarUrl}`} />;
}

// ── AFTER: Fixed type + runtime safety ──
interface Contact {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  email: string;
  phone: string;
  avatarUrl: string | null;  // FIXED: nullable
}

// Runtime validation with Zod
import { z } from 'zod';
const ContactSchema = z.object({
  id: z.string(),
  name: z.string(),
  email: z.string().email(),
  phone: z.string(),
  avatarUrl: z.string().nullable()
});
type Contact = z.infer<typeof ContactSchema>;

// Safe API client with runtime validation
const apiClient = {
  contacts: {
    getById: async (id: string): Promise<Contact> => {
      const res = await fetch(`/api/contacts/${id}`);
      const data = await res.json();
      return ContactSchema.parse(data); // Throws if shape mismatch
    }
  }
};

// Safe component
function ContactAvatar({ contact }: { contact: Contact }) {
  if (!contact.avatarUrl) return <DefaultAvatar name={contact.name} />;
  const isExternal = contact.avatarUrl.startsWith('http');
  return <img src={isExternal ? contact.avatarUrl : `/uploads/${contact.avatarUrl}`} />;
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

In ContactsHub, Manoj got a Sentry error report: "Cannot read properties of null (reading 'startsWith')" from the ContactAvatar component in production. His debugging process: (1) Checked the component code — TypeScript showed no errors because avatarUrl was typed as string. (2) Opened the Network tab in DevTools, fetched a contact that was created without an image upload, and saw "avatarUrl": null in the JSON response. (3) Traced back to the Fastify handler: when Cloudinary upload was skipped (no file attached), the handler set avatarUrl: null. (4) The root cause was the typed API client promising string when the server could return null. (5) Fixed by updating the interface to string | null, which immediately surfaced 4 other components that needed null checks — TypeScript showed compile-time errors at each location. (6) Added Zod validation in the API client to catch future type mismatches at runtime with clear error messages instead of cryptic property-access crashes.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpactMitigation
API client types not matching actual server responsesRuntime crashes on null/undefined fieldsUse Zod schemas to validate responses; generate types from schemas
Using res.json() without validation — returns anyNo compile-time safety on the actual dataWrap every fetch call with a schema parser
Server adding new optional fields not reflected in client typesClient ignores useful data or breaks on unexpected shapesShare types via a shared package or generate from OpenAPI spec
Error responses typed the same as success responsesCode tries to access data.name on an error objectUse discriminated unions: { success: true, data } | { success: false, error }
Forgetting to handle network errors in typed clientUnhandled promise rejections crash the appAdd try/catch wrapper with typed error responses in the API client

7. Validation Strategy

  • Contract testing: Write tests that call the real Fastify handler and parse the response through the same Zod schema the client uses — if the schema changes, tests fail
  • Playwright E2E: Test the ContactsHub contact creation flow both with and without an avatar image, verifying the UI renders correctly in both cases
  • Null-path unit tests: For every component that receives API data, test with null/undefined optional fields to verify graceful handling
  • TypeScript strict null checks: Ensure strictNullChecks: true in tsconfig so the compiler forces null handling everywhere

Comparing Two Type-safe API Client Approaches with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

Two dominant approaches to type-safe API clients in TypeScript are: Approach A — Hand-written typed wrappers, where developers manually create typed functions around fetch or axios with explicit interfaces for each endpoint; and Approach B — Code-generated clients from OpenAPI/Swagger specs, where a tool like openapi-typescript or swagger-typescript-api reads the API specification and auto-generates fully typed client code. Each has distinct trade-offs around maintenance burden, type accuracy, developer experience, and build complexity.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub & HR Leave System

In Manoj's ContactsHub (React + Fastify), he used hand-written typed wrappers. The API surface was small (6 endpoints: CRUD contacts + image upload + search), so manually typing each method was straightforward. Each wrapper function explicitly declared its input and output types, and Manoj added Zod validation on responses. In the HR Leave Management System, the API surface was larger (15+ endpoints across leaves, employees, approvals, notifications). Here, hand-written wrappers became a maintenance burden — every time a Prisma schema change altered the response shape, Manoj had to update the Fastify handler, the shared interface, AND the Angular service type. A code-generated approach from an OpenAPI spec would have automated the Angular service types, reducing the sync burden from three places to two.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// ═══ APPROACH A: Hand-written Typed Wrapper (ContactsHub) ═══

interface Contact {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  email: string;
  phone: string;
  avatarUrl: string | null;
}

interface CreateContactInput {
  name: string;
  email: string;
  phone: string;
  avatar?: File;
}

class ContactsApi {
  private baseUrl = '/api/contacts';

  async create(input: CreateContactInput): Promise<Contact> {
    const formData = new FormData();
    formData.append('name', input.name);
    formData.append('email', input.email);
    formData.append('phone', input.phone);
    if (input.avatar) formData.append('avatar', input.avatar);

    const res = await fetch(this.baseUrl, { method: 'POST', body: formData });
    if (!res.ok) throw new ApiError(res.status);
    return res.json() as Promise<Contact>;
  }

  async getAll(): Promise<Contact[]> {
    const res = await fetch(this.baseUrl);
    return res.json() as Promise<Contact[]>;
  }
}

// ═══ APPROACH B: Code-generated from OpenAPI (HR Leave System) ═══

// Step 1: Fastify auto-generates OpenAPI spec via @fastify/swagger
// Step 2: Run codegen
// npx openapi-typescript http://localhost:3000/docs/json -o src/api/schema.d.ts

// Step 3: Use generated types
import type { paths } from './api/schema';
import createClient from 'openapi-fetch';

const client = createClient<paths>({ baseUrl: '/api' });

// Fully typed — path, method, body, and response all inferred
const { data, error } = await client.POST('/leaves', {
  body: {
    employeeId: 'emp-123',
    startDate: '2025-03-01',
    endDate: '2025-03-05',
    leaveType: 'casual',
    reason: 'Family event'
  }
});
// data is typed as LeaveResponse automatically
// error is typed as ErrorResponse automatically

// Adding a new endpoint? Just regenerate:
// npx openapi-typescript http://localhost:3000/docs/json -o src/api/schema.d.ts
// All types update automatically — zero manual work

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj experienced the pain point that motivated considering code-generated clients. He added a halfDay boolean field to the Prisma leave schema. After running prisma generate, the Fastify handler and Prisma types were automatically updated. However, the Angular frontend's LeaveService still used the old LeaveRequest interface without halfDay. The leave form submitted data without the field, and PostgreSQL inserted null (since the column was nullable). No compile error, no runtime error — just silently lost data. Manoj only discovered the issue when a manager asked why half-day leaves were not tracked. The fix was adding halfDay to the Angular interface, but the deeper realization was that with 15+ endpoints, manually keeping three type sources in sync (Prisma schema, Fastify types, Angular interfaces) was unsustainable. For future projects of this scale, Manoj decided to use OpenAPI codegen to eliminate the Angular type maintenance entirely.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpactMitigation
Hand-written types drifting from actual API responsesSilent data loss or runtime crashesAdd runtime validation (Zod) in the API client layer
Generated types becoming stale (not regenerated after API change)Compile-time types no longer match realityAdd codegen step to CI pipeline; fail build if spec changed but types not regenerated
OpenAPI spec not accurately reflecting actual behaviorGenerated types are wrong; false sense of safetyUse Fastify's JSON Schema integration with @fastify/swagger for spec accuracy
Code-generated client being too rigid for custom needsComplex requests (file uploads, streaming) need workaroundsAllow escape hatches: extend the generated client with custom methods
Over-engineering a small API with codegen toolchainBuild complexity not justified for 5-6 endpointsUse hand-written wrappers for small projects; codegen for 10+ endpoints

7. Validation Strategy

  • Contract tests: For hand-written clients, write tests that call the server and validate response shapes against the TypeScript interface using Zod
  • Codegen freshness check: CI step that regenerates types and fails if the git diff shows changes — meaning someone changed the API without regenerating
  • E2E coverage: Playwright tests in ContactsHub that exercise every API endpoint through the UI, catching any type mismatch that survives compile-time
  • Type coverage measurement: Use type-coverage npm package to ensure >95% of values are typed (not any)

Debugging a Real TS Config Issue

1. Definition & Context

tsconfig.json is the configuration file that controls how the TypeScript compiler (tsc) processes your project. Key settings include strict (enables all strict type-checking options), target (output JavaScript version), module (module system — CommonJS, ESNext, NodeNext), moduleResolution (how imports are resolved), paths (import aliases), baseUrl, outDir, rootDir, include/exclude patterns, and references for project references. Debugging tsconfig issues is notoriously frustrating because errors often manifest as cryptic messages like "Cannot find module" or "Module has no exported member" even when the file clearly exists.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the Fastify backend used a monorepo-like structure with shared types between the server and utility modules. Manoj configured path aliases in tsconfig: "@shared/*": ["src/shared/*"] with baseUrl: ".". TypeScript compilation (tsc --noEmit) passed without errors. However, at runtime, Node.js threw Error: Cannot find module '@shared/types'. The issue: TypeScript path aliases are a compile-time only feature — they do not modify the emitted JavaScript require()/import paths. Node.js has no knowledge of tsconfig path mappings. Manoj needed a runtime path resolver like tsconfig-paths or module-alias to bridge the gap.

3. Debugging Flow Diagram

4. Code Example

// ── tsconfig.json with path aliases ──
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2022",
    "module": "NodeNext",
    "moduleResolution": "NodeNext",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "src",
    "baseUrl": ".",
    "paths": {
      "@shared/*": ["src/shared/*"],
      "@routes/*": ["src/routes/*"],
      "@middleware/*": ["src/middleware/*"]
    }
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*"]
}

// ── Source code using alias ──
import { LeaveRequest } from '@shared/types';  // Works in tsc
import { authGuard } from '@middleware/auth';   // Works in tsc

// ── Compiled output (dist/routes/leaves.js) ──
// const { LeaveRequest } = require('@shared/types');  // FAILS at runtime!
// Node.js doesn't know what @shared means

// ═══ FIX OPTION 1: tsconfig-paths (for development with ts-node) ═══
// package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "ts-node -r tsconfig-paths/register src/server.ts",
    "build": "tsc && tsc-alias"  // tsc-alias rewrites paths in compiled JS
  }
}
// npm install -D tsconfig-paths tsc-alias

// ═══ FIX OPTION 2: Use moduleResolution: NodeNext with relative imports ═══
// Replace alias imports with relative paths
import { LeaveRequest } from '../shared/types.js';  // Note: .js extension!
import { authGuard } from '../middleware/auth.js';

// ═══ FIX OPTION 3 (chosen by Manoj): Fastify with tsx runner ═══
// tsx natively supports tsconfig paths
{
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "tsx watch src/server.ts"  // tsx resolves paths automatically
  }
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj hit this issue when transitioning the HR Leave System's Fastify backend from JavaScript to TypeScript. He set up path aliases to clean up deeply nested relative imports like ../../../shared/types. The code compiled perfectly — tsc --noEmit showed zero errors. But running node dist/server.js immediately crashed with Cannot find module '@shared/types'. Debugging steps: (1) He checked the compiled JS output and saw the import paths were unchanged — still @shared/types. (2) He Googled the issue and learned TypeScript intentionally does not rewrite import paths — it only uses paths for type resolution. (3) He tried tsconfig-paths with ts-node, which worked in development but not for the compiled production build. (4) He discovered tsc-alias, a post-compilation tool that rewrites the import paths in the emitted JS to relative paths. (5) Final solution: used tsx for development (which handles paths natively) and tsc && tsc-alias for the production build. The key takeaway: tsconfig paths is a compiler feature, not a runtime feature.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpactMitigation
Assuming paths aliases work at runtimeProduction builds crash on module resolutionUse tsc-alias or tsx to resolve aliases at runtime
Mixing module: CommonJS with ESM-only packagesERR_REQUIRE_ESM at runtimeUse module: NodeNext and add .js extensions to imports
Setting strict: false to fix errors quicklyDisables critical checks like strictNullChecksEnable strict: true and fix errors one by one
Mismatched rootDir and outDir causing wrong output pathsCompiled files end up in unexpected directoriesSet rootDir: "src" and outDir: "dist" explicitly
Forgetting include/exclude — compiling test files or node_modulesBuild includes test stubs; slow compilationSet "include": ["src"] and "exclude": ["node_modules", "dist", "**/*.test.ts"]

7. Validation Strategy

  • Build smoke test: CI runs tsc && node dist/server.js --dry-run to verify the compiled output starts without module resolution errors
  • Path alias verification: Write a script that checks every @alias/ import in the compiled JS and verifies the resolved file exists on disk
  • tsconfig linting: Use @tsconfig/node18 recommended base to avoid misconfiguration; extend rather than write from scratch
  • Docker build test: Build the production Docker image and run the health check endpoint to verify end-to-end module resolution

Comparing Two TS Config Approaches with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

Two common approaches to TypeScript configuration in multi-package or full-stack projects are: Approach A — Single tsconfig with broad settings, where one tsconfig.json at the project root governs all TypeScript code (frontend + backend); and Approach B — Multiple tsconfigs with project references, where each part of the codebase (server, client, shared) has its own tsconfig.json that extends a common base, connected via TypeScript's references feature for incremental builds. The choice depends on project size, build performance needs, and team structure.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub vs HR Leave System

In ContactsHub, Manoj used a single tsconfig because the project was a straightforward React frontend with a separate Fastify backend in a different repository. One tsconfig per repo was sufficient — the React app had its own tsconfig.json with jsx: "react-jsx", and the Fastify backend had its own with module: "NodeNext". In the HR Leave System, both Angular frontend and Fastify backend lived in a more tightly coupled structure with shared type definitions. Manoj initially used a single root tsconfig but ran into problems: Angular needed module: "ES2022" while Fastify needed module: "NodeNext"; Angular needed jsx excluded while the shared types needed to be accessible by both. He refactored into multiple tsconfigs: tsconfig.base.json (shared strict settings), tsconfig.server.json (extends base, NodeNext module), and tsconfig.app.json (extends base, Angular settings).

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// ═══ APPROACH A: Single tsconfig (ContactsHub React app) ═══
// tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2022",
    "lib": ["ES2022", "DOM", "DOM.Iterable"],
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "jsx": "react-jsx",
    "strict": true,
    "noUnusedLocals": true,
    "noUnusedParameters": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "outDir": "dist"
  },
  "include": ["src"]
}

// ═══ APPROACH B: Multiple tsconfigs (HR Leave System) ═══

// tsconfig.base.json — shared strict settings
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "declaration": true,
    "declarationMap": true,
    "sourceMap": true
  }
}

// packages/shared/tsconfig.json — shared types
{
  "extends": "../../tsconfig.base.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2022",
    "module": "ESNext",
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "src",
    "composite": true  // Required for project references
  },
  "include": ["src"]
}

// server/tsconfig.json — Fastify backend
{
  "extends": "../tsconfig.base.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2022",
    "module": "NodeNext",
    "moduleResolution": "NodeNext",
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "src"
  },
  "references": [
    { "path": "../packages/shared" }
  ],
  "include": ["src"]
}

// Root tsconfig.json — orchestrator
{
  "files": [],
  "references": [
    { "path": "packages/shared" },
    { "path": "server" }
  ]
}

// Build command: tsc --build (incremental, respects references)
// Only recompiles changed projects

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

When Manoj added shared TypeScript types between the Angular frontend and Fastify backend in the HR Leave System, he initially tried importing from a shared directory using a single root tsconfig. Angular's ng build failed because it uses its own tsconfig (tsconfig.app.json) and does not respect the root paths configuration. Meanwhile, the Fastify server needed module: "NodeNext" for proper ESM support, but Angular required module: "ES2022". The single tsconfig could not serve both. Manoj's solution: (1) Created tsconfig.base.json with only the strict settings both environments shared. (2) Angular's tsconfig.app.json extended the base with Angular-specific settings. (3) The server's tsconfig.json extended the base with Node-specific settings. (4) The shared types package had its own tsconfig with composite: true for project references. (5) Used tsc --build for the server, which incrementally compiled only changed projects. Build time dropped from 12 seconds to 3 seconds on typical changes because the shared package was only recompiled when its source changed.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpactMitigation
Forgetting composite: true in referenced projectstsc --build fails with cryptic "project references require composite"Always add composite: true and declaration: true to referenced projects
Circular references between projectsBuild hangs or fails with "circular reference" errorDesign a strict dependency tree: shared → server, shared → client; never server → client
IDE not picking up the right tsconfig for a fileVS Code shows false errors or misses real onesUse "solution" style root tsconfig with only references; configure typescript.tsdk
Extending base config but overriding strict in childOne project silently loses strict checksNever override strict in child configs; keep it in base only
Using include patterns that overlap between configsSame file compiled by two configs with different settingsEnsure each tsconfig's include covers a non-overlapping directory

7. Validation Strategy

  • CI type-check per project: Run tsc --noEmit -p server/tsconfig.json and ng build --configuration=production separately in CI to catch config-specific errors
  • Config lint: Write a script that parses all tsconfig files and asserts strict: true is present in the base and not overridden in children
  • Build time monitoring: Log tsc --build --diagnostics output in CI; alert if build time regresses beyond threshold
  • Project reference integrity: Script that verifies every references entry points to a directory containing a tsconfig with composite: true
